,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1683,"Africana Studies",13579,Minor,Non-Degree,AFDST.MIN,"Africana Studies Minor","

An interdisciplinary minor, Africana studies is an integrative program intended to deepen students' understanding of a region of the world, and of widely dispersed cultural traditions, that are of increasing significance to global economics, health policy, international development, and Christian theology. A broad choice of courses is offered, making it possible to adapt the minor to a variety of major programs in the humanities, social sciences, and fine arts. The minor program encompasses study both of Africa and its peoples and of the dispersal of Africans to Europe and the Americas through forced migration and voluntary immigration. E. Washington (Historical Studies) is the advisor for this program.

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1684,Archaeology,13580,Minor,Non-Degree,ARCH.MIN,"Archaeology Minor","

For this interdisciplinary minor, in addition to two required courses, students may select a coherent set of at least three elective courses with the help and approval of an advisor in the minor program. This selection should be appropriate to their major and in keeping with their chosen interests, specialized skills, and plans for further study. Such a program design could stress specialized interests such as material analysis or computer graphics among others and choices from various fields in old world, new world, or marine archaeology for which field schools are available.

There are no modern language requirements for the archaeology minor, but students are strongly encouraged to study an appropriate modern language. For Old World archaeology the best modern language choice is French or German, while Spanish is useful for much of New World archaeology.

The minor in archaeology is administered by D. Rohl (Historical Studies). Interested students should consult with him for admission to and planning of the archaeology minor.

","Archaeology Minor

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1713,"Visual and Performing Arts",13583,Major,"Bachelor of Fine Arts",GRDSN.MAJ,"Graphic Design Major, BFA","

The graphic design major equips students for professional and personal opportunities in the world of design and related fields, by developing skills to solve foundational design problems (whatever the medium) as embedded in a way of thinking. Typography, as the arrangement and handling of type to make language visible, is a central focus of the program, along with the formation of students' own tactics for concept development within a design context. Supported by foundational studio courses such as drawing and photography, along with course options from the communication and business departments, the major takes a broad approach to exploring the fundamental role of design as an essential part of our commercial and social contexts, indeed as an integral part of the human experience. (The program requires students to have a laptop and a subscription to the Adobe Suite; however, relative to courses in other programs, costs for resources such as books and studio materials are minimal.)

This program may not be combined with any other studio art or graphic design major or minor.

","Graphic Design Major, BFA

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1685,"Asian Studies",13586,Major,"Bachelor of Arts",ASNST.MAJ,"Asian Studies, BA","

The Asian studies major is designed for students developing a strong background in Asian languages and historical, cultural, and ideological understandings of Asian countries as preparation for various professions, including international development, business, education, and missions. Beyond exploring the philosophy, religion, art, and history of Asian cultures and nations in classrooms, majors have opportunities to take a summer and semester program in Taiwan, Japan, or Korea, and summer off-campus programs in India, Nepal, Thailand, the Philippines, or Cambodia. The Asian studies program is structured to fit nicely alongside other majors and minors for enhancing thoughtful engagement with the diverse peoples and cultures of Asia. Admission to the major program requires completion of a core course with a minimum grade of C (2.0). Contact W. Lee (Religion) for more information.

Students considering this minor should contact the biotechnology advisor, D. Koetje.

The college's course overlap policy states that students must have at least 14 unique 3+-credit courses between major and minor programs. Cognate courses do not factor into this total. ,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",S,1716,"School of Business",13594,Major,"Bachelor of Science in Accounting",ACCT.MAJ,"Accounting, BSA","

Accounting is the means by which organizations communicate financial information. As a result, accounting is often called the ""language of business."" Students completing an accounting major have a variety of career options from which to choose.

Studying accounting includes learning about financial accounting—the reporting of an organization's financial information—and managerial accounting—the use of that data to measure the entity's performance and inform decisions about its future. An accounting major learns how organizations' financial records are prepared and maintained. Accounting includes the study of accounting systems, auditing, financial reporting and analysis, and taxation.

The bachelor of science in accounting (BSA) degree is designed to be completed in four years. Students will have the opportunity to complete off-campus internships in various accounting roles. Students may continue their studies at Calvin in the master of accounting (MAcc) program to complete the educational requirements of the Certified Public Accountant (CPA) licensure.

This program may not be combined with the business minor program. No more than two majors from the School of Business may be obtained.

","Accounting, BSA

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1710,"Speech Pathology and Audiology",13598,Master's,"Master of Arts",SPATH.MAJ,"Speech-Language Pathology, MA","

The MA in speech-language pathology is an entry-level degree to practice as a speech-language pathologist. The MA in speech-language pathology program offers two different modalities: the residential (on-campus) mode and the distance education mode. Students enrolled in either one or the other should register for the classes offered for their modality, either on-campus for the residential modality or online for the distance education modality. Residential modality students will not be allowed to take a class online without permission from the program director. Students who have registered for a course in the other modality will be automatically un-enrolled from the course at such time that their enrollment is discovered, which may delay completion of the course, loss of tuition, and a delay in clinical placements or even graduation. Residential modality students are expected to attend classes in person as scheduled. Students in the residential mode are expected to complete the program in two years. The distance-education mode allows students to complete the program in a two-year or three-year track. The three-year track is strongly recommended for most students due to their non-academic time commitments.

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",S,1717,"School of Health",13602,,Non-Degree,PROPT,"Pre-professional: Optometry","

Students wishing to become optometrists complete a BA or BS degree at Calvin before entering optometry school to complete four additional years of study culminating in the doctor of optometry (OD) degree. Any student interested in applying to optometry programs should consult R. Bebej (Biology).
A student may select nearly any major and still meet entrance requirements for most optometry programs. However, the majority of applicants to optometry programs are science majors.

Prerequisite courses vary among optometry programs, and students should consult with R. Bebej, the School of Health advising office (pre-health@calvin.edu), and the website for the Association of Schools and Colleges of Optometry (opted.org) to confirm admission requirements at different schools. We recommend that students take the following courses to prepare for most optometry programs: BIOL 141 or BIOL 161; BIOL 205 or BIOL 323; BIOL 206 or BIOL 231-BIOL 331; BIOL 207 or BIOL 336; CHEM 101-CHEM 102; CHEM 240 or CHEM 241-CHEM 242; PHYS 221-PHYS 222 or PHYS 133-PHYS 235; STAT 143 or STAT 145; MATH 132, MATH 161, or MATH 162; and PSYC 151. Some schools may also recommend a business or economics course.

Pre-optometry students take the Optometry Admission Test once they have completed the recommended coursework and determined when they have the time to fully prepare for the test. They should meet with the pre-optometry and pre-health advisors to learn more about shadowing and observation hour requirements, clinical experience, and other activities that strengthen their application. Calvin offers an elective course that identifies many of these requirements: IDIS 106 - Introduction to Health Professions.

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",S,1717,"School of Health",13605,,Non-Degree,PRPHT,"Pre-professional: Physical Therapy","

Students wishing to enter the field of physical therapy must complete a doctoral degree in physical therapy (DPT). Students at Calvin can prepare to complete this degree by completing the prerequisite courses for their programs of interest in conjunction with a degree program in any discipline. Students then attend graduate school. Admission to graduate programs in physical therapy is very competitive.

The prerequisite courses depend on the graduate school to which students wish to apply; therefore, students should obtain a list of requirements for each of the graduate schools in which they are interested. Below is a sample list of prerequisite classes for non-biology majors. Many students take the courses listed below as part of the Kinesiology Major: Pre-professional Emphasis, BS. Students are encouraged to consult with the School of Health advising office (pre-health@calvin.edu) before they register for classes. Students must also work or volunteer with patients under the supervision of a licensed physical therapist. Fifty to one hundred hours of shadowing is required for application to most DPT programs. Students should meet with the pre-health advisors to learn more about shadowing and other activities that strengthen their application. Calvin offers an elective course that identifies many of these requirements: IDIS 106 - Introduction to Health Professions.

","Pre-professional: Physical Therapy

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1708,"Sociology and Social Work",13606,Major,"Bachelor of Social Work",SOWK.MAJ,"Social Work Major, BSW","

The bachelor of social work degree is designed for students who want to prepare for a career of Christian service as a professional generalist social worker. The program is carried out in the context of the mission of Calvin, which is to offer a Christian education enriched by the insights of the Reformed heritage. Upon completion of the program, students are prepared for entry-level professional social work positions. The BSW program is accredited by the Council on Social Work Education.

Students who wish to pursue a BSW will normally apply to the director of social work by April 1 of their sophomore year. Decisions about admission to the program are made by the social work program committee and are based on the following criteria:

column2: • Students must have earned at least 35 semester hours of credit and have completed PSYC 151, SOC 151, and SOWK 240
• Students must have a minimum grade point average of 2.5 and a minimum grade of C- in each of the courses just specified
• Students must have completed at least 30 hours of social work volunteer or paid service (from no more than two settings) and submit a letter of reference from an appropriate supervisor

• Students must submit a written personal statement, which includes information about their commitment to social work as a vocation and their relative strengths and areas for development as potential professional social workers.

column1: The BSW is composed of twelve courses and several social science cognate requirements. The social work major includes courses in social work theory, practice skills, policy analysis, and research, along with a field education experience in a human service agency. Once admitted to the BSW program, students must apply separately to field education at the beginning of their junior year. Student retention and degree completion require a minimum grade of C- in all 200-level social work courses and a C or above in all 300-level social work courses. The core requirements include the liberal arts offerings required of all students, with SOC 151, PSYC 151, BIOL 115, and SOC 255/SOWK 255 required as social work cognates.

A more detailed description of the BSW program is given in the Social Work Handbook, which can be obtained at the department office or online.

","Social Work Major, BSW

The neuroscience concentration in psychology seeks to provide students with an interdisciplinary understanding of neuroscience, giving them a deep understanding of the biological sciences and integrating this knowledge with diverse subject areas such as psychology, chemistry/biochemistry, physics, computer science, and philosophy. This training prepares students for graduate school in neuroscience and professional fields (e.g., medicine, dentistry, clinical psychology, veterinary medicine, physical therapy), as well as careers in biotechnology firms, government laboratories, pharmaceutical companies, education, law, and business. Students desiring greater focus on biological, chemical or physical aspects of neuroscience should consider the neuroscience concentrations offered through the chemistry and biology departments.

","Psychology Major: Neuroscience Concentration, BS

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1705,Psychology,13615,Major,"Bachelor of Arts",PSYC.MAJ,"Psychology Major, BA","

Students must maintain a 2.0 in psychology courses in order to graduate with a major or minor in psychology.
Psychology majors who are also ministry minors are advised to take PSYC 201.
Students should ordinarily take PSYC 255 during their sophomore year and PSYC 256 in the semester following completion of PSYC 255. Students may not take PSYC 255 and PSYC 256 simultaneously.

When possible, students are encouraged to postpone taking courses numbered 330 or above until after the completion of PSYC 256. PSYC 399 is a course that may be taken when the student has completed a majority of the psychology courses required for the psychology major.

Students intending to do doctoral work in psychology are strongly encouraged to take PSYC 356 during their junior or senior year. When selecting courses, students are encouraged to follow the recommended four-year plan for the general psychology major or the plan for majors with a neuroscience concentration. A four-year plan is available in the psychology office.

The psychology department offers a bachelor of arts (BA) degree in psychology and a Bachelor of Science (BS) degree in psychology. The BS course of study has stronger quantitative and research components. It is recommended that students intent on graduate study in psychology complete the coursework for the BS degree. 

","Psychology Major, BA

Majoring in philosophy is your opportunity to join a community devoted to exploring questions of enduring significance, from human nature and our knowledge of God to the meaning of life and lifelong formation in virtue. ""Why?"" is our favorite question. Learn to approach conversations and engage culture with boldness, charity, careful discernment, and curiosity.

Philosophy is the lived pursuit of wisdom in everyday life—from your spiritual vocation to your profession to your personal relationships. By majoring in philosophy at Calvin, you will cultivate foundational academic skills that give you a distinctive edge in career preparation, all in the larger context of a flourishing human life.

The philosophy department welcomes ""undecideds,"" doubters, and explorers to join us in the quest for wisdom. Our program is intentionally flexible for double majors and offers a way to deepen any field of study.

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1680,"Off-Campus Programs",13640,"Off-Campus Programs",Non-Degree,STFR,"Study in France (STFR)","

The Study in France program takes place every other fall semester (even years) in Grenoble, a mid-sized city located in a valley of the French Alps. Students direct-enroll at the Centre universitaire d'études françaises (CUEF), a French language and culture institute that is part of the Université Grenoble Alpes.

Students live with French host families in Grenoble. Students take one course with the Calvin program director and the equivalent of three French language and culture courses at the CUEF. Students participate in group activities as well as excursions to locations such as Paris, Lyon, and Marseille.

When students successfully complete their courses at the CUEF, they will receive transfer credit for STFR 315, STFR 316, and STFR 381. The CUEF will also issue a formal certificate of their French level according to the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFRL) and the A1 to C2 scale.

While the program focuses on advanced-level students preparing a French major or minor, beginner and intermediate students may also apply to the program. According to their level and performance at the CUEF, these students may obtain Core, elective, and potentially major or minor credit in French (16 semester hours total). For details, contact the program advisor for fall 2024, Professor Jolene Vos-Camy.

Academic components of the program include the following:

","Study in France (STFR)

* STFR 316 - Advanced Language Study in France II

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1715,"World Languages",13649,Minor,Non-Degree,NETH.MAJ,"Dutch Studies Minor","

Recognizing the richness of Dutch language, culture, and heritage, Calvin University has designed a Netherlandic Studies minor to fit well with many different majors at Calvin. In this program students learn how such a small country came to have such an over-sized impact on global trade, the arts, science, and democratic social norms. In addition to gaining some proficiency in the Dutch language and culture, students will gain insights from Dutch perspectives on religious freedom and tolerance that may surprise them. They explore how the Dutch became renown globally for their collective entrepreneurial spirit in the face of persistent environmental challenges from flood protection to urban design to food production, and how these are now inspiring circular economies and nature-based solutions in the Netherlands. Finally, students have an opportunity to experience all of this for themselves in a 3-week summer course in the Netherlands led by an interdisciplinary team of Calvin faculty.

Students interested in this program need to contact the chair of the Department of World Languages.

","Dutch Studies Minor

The bachelor of science (BS) in economics program provides students with thorough training in both theoretical and empirical approaches to understanding issues such as unemployment, inflation, taxes, healthcare, poverty, inequality, pollution, crime, and international trade. It is specifically designed for students who want experience in quantitative analysis for their future careers and for those who plan to attend graduate school in economics, finance, or related fields. The program focuses on equipping students with quantitative skills required to address contemporary economic problems. The BS in economics degree is a STEM-designated degree program and provides international students with three years of optional practical training (OPT).

This program may not be combined with the politics, philosophy, and economics major program.

","Economics Major, BS

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1700,"Classical and Medieval Studies",13656,Minor,Non-Degree,MDVST.MIN,"Classical and Medieval Studies Minor","

The interdisciplinary minor in classical and medieval studies aims to inspire students both to appreciate and to examine critically the ancient and medieval roots of modern culture. It is a useful introduction to graduate study in any of the disciplines in which classical and medieval studies play a role (e.g., classics, medieval studies, history, religion, art history, music, modern languages). The minor may be taken in conjunction with any major.
This minor requires a minimum of 18 semester hours. In addition to the interdisciplinary seminar (2 hours), one course must be taken in history, and one in an ancient or modern European language.

The minor in classical and medieval studies is administered by F. Van Liere (Historical Studies). Interested students should consult with him for selection of specific courses for the minor.

","Classical and Medieval Studies Minor

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1698,Kinesiology,13670,Minor,Non-Degree,RECR.MIN,"Recreation Leadership Minor","

All students interested in developing their abilities to lead, facilitate, program, instruct, and manage risk are encouraged to complete a minor in recreation leadership Studies during their time at Calvin University. Students selecting the recreation leadership minor will experience the Calvin campus as a learning lab. Along with on-campus courses, students travel well beyond the traditional classroom setting, exploring the rich and powerful experiential context of national and global field courses, community based internships, adventure programming, and/or wilderness-based learning contexts. Recreation leadership minors will develop proficiency in a highly transferable leader skill sets; group facilitation, instruction, programming, risk management, development of teams, management of group dynamics, conflict management and resolution, and development of individual self-efficacy.
The recreation leadership minor exists to empower Calvin students to

column2: • Develop a philosophy of leadership based in virtue ethics and distinct Christian perspectives;
• understand the transformative impact of good leadership in the lives of individuals and groups;
• create a servant leadership disposition centered in stewardship, empathy, and individual development;
• cultivate a signature style of leading that capitalizes on assets and strengths and develops liabilities;
• be a part of collaborative leadership projects that bring curriculum to life, through real-world projects;
• build and test leader-craft through leader roles in outcomes-based opportunities and programs; and
• build leadership efficacy; competence and confidence, though complex skill acquisition.

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1694,English,13674,Major,"Bachelor of Arts",LIT.MAJ,"English Major: Literature Concentration, BA","

The English department offers an English major with four concentrations—linguistics, literature, writing, and general English studies—that effectively anchors earning in the integrated art, craft, and science of language. The major provides students a richly faceted set of skills with which to engage story, practice interpretation, and experience the cultural and historical diversity of language and literature. Students majoring in literature focus primarily on story, poetry, and/or drama—on the ways language shapes and reflects human experience. This concentration strengthens a reader's attentive reading and analytical skills—excellent preparation for a number of careers, including law, business, editing, writing, library science, teaching, ministry, or public relations.
ENGL 395 , 2 semester hours of electives, and experiential requirement (ENGL 380, 399 or HNRS 399) are waived for students double majoring in English and another subject.

University policy does not allow students to pursue a major in more than one English concentration.

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1692,Education,13681,Minor,Non-Degree,MIN_ESL_ED,"Teaching English to Speakers of Other Languages (TESOL) Minor: Certification Track","

The TESOL minor prepares students to (1) promote educational equity and the academic achievement of learners whose home language is not English; (2) deliver instruction in how to speak, read, write, listen, and communicate in English; and (3) support learners in their acquisition of content knowledge and academic language while they learn English.

Students in the Education program must combine the TESOL minor with at least one of the following programs: elementary education (PK-3 or 3-6), or any of the following 5-9, 7-12, or PK-12 programs: English language arts, French, mathematics, science, social studies, Spanish, or special education. Secondary candidates should consider the English language arts major or minor. Students must complete Calvin's world language core.

The program leads to the PK-12 English as a Second Language endorsement on the Michigan teaching certificate. Students who are not in the education program should complete the TESOL non-certification minor. Students in the education program complete one of the following degree options for teacher certification:

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1697,"Geology and Geography",13684,Major,"Bachelor of Arts",ENST.GEOG.MAJ,"Environmental Sustainability and Geography, BA: Sustainability Concentration","

The environmental sustainability and geography major encompasses the natural sciences, social sciences, and humanities in order to give students a broad understanding of the relationships between people, places, and the environment. Graduates of this program are prepared to understand complex environmental and societal issues and to promote the flourishing of communities, regions, and the environment. Majors choose one of two concentrations: geography or sustainability. Students choosing the geography concentration often enter careers as GIS analysts for governments or corporations, city and regional planners, or land-use planners and managers. Students choosing the sustainability concentration follow careers ranging from environmental law to environmental management to sustainability officers for governments and corporations. Students majoring in environmental sustainability and geography are prepared for graduate study in urban planning, environmental sustainability, geography, and public administration.

","Environmental Sustainability and Geography, BA: Sustainability Concentration

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1714,"Historical Studies",13690,Major,"Bachelor of Arts",HIST.MAJ,"History, BA","

The history major is an excellent liberal arts major for students wishing to deepen their understanding of the world and its diverse cultures, past and present. Majors will develop valuable and highly transferable skills in research, textual and cultural analysis, and oral and written rhetoric. These provide an excellent foundation for vocations such as law, journalism, ministry, library science, business, and teaching.

History majors are encouraged to consider minors in closely related fields such as Africana studies, archaeology, classical and medieval studies, or gender studies, as well as minors in more diverse areas.

The history major requires two specific courses on the methods and practice of history; ideally these are taken in the first year (HIST 294) and senior year (historiography capstone). In between these two endpoints, History majors choose their own program of courses according to their particular interests, within certain guidelines that ensure chronological, geographical, cultural, and disciplinary diversity.

One elective (4 semester hours) is waived for students double majoring in history and another subject.

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",I,1677,"Calvin University",13700,,,,"The Knight Scholars Program","

The Knight Scholars Program resources students in their transition to learning at Calvin University.  Knight Scholar status is granted by the Committee on Admissions to those students who show promise to succeed academically at Calvin and to contribute to the community.  Admission is based on high school grades, high school coursework, standardized test scores, recommendations, and related admissions materials.

The Knight Scholars Program supports students through proactive advising, personalized coursework, and engaged coaching. Students meet with their advisor before their first semester at Calvin to identify available opportunities that will help them make a smooth transition to college learning and living. Students often choose to take coursework on college learning, foundational mathematics, and enhanced written rhetoric. Other common supports include advanced access to coaches and tutors, regular communications about academic details, and help selecting a major

TRIO Student Support Services partners with first-generation students, students from low-income families, and students with disabilities by providing academic and holistic support to enable flourishing from day one of college through graduation. TRIO SSS is federally funded to provide students with enhanced academic support as well as free workshops, resources and activities promoting success in and beyond college. In addition, the TRIO SSS program encourages involvement in TRIO cohort activities designed to foster connection with other TRIO SSS participants and foster belonging at Calvin (e.g., off-campus cultural excursions, spring break trip, graduate school visits and other activities). Students can apply to the TRIO SSS program through the Calvin website and must be a US citizen or noncitizen who is eligible for federal student financial aid.

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",I,1677,"Calvin University",13701,,,,"The Rhetoric across the Curriculum Program","

The Rhetoric across the Curriculum (RAC) program fosters student engagement with writing, speaking, and information literacy in the disciplines. Students enter the program through the Calvin Core curriculum, taking foundational courses in written, oral, and visual rhetoric. They continue through their major program, taking rhetoric-intensive courses that emphasize critical rhetorical practices in the discipline.

Rhetoric-Intensive Courses: Departments designate courses in their curriculum as rhetoric intensive. Designating courses as rhetoric intensive ensures that students receive rhetorical instruction in the discipline, and such courses serve as a locus for assessment.
Transfer Students: Students who transfer into a major program should work with their major advisor to determine what they must do to satisfy RAC requirements.

Minimum Grade in English 101 or 102: As the first step in developing competence in written rhetoric, a minimum grade of C is required of all students receiving credit for ENGL 101 or ENGL 102.

","The Rhetoric across the Curriculum Program",2,,,,,,,,"2025-07-21 12:10:45","2025-07-21 12:10:45"

Program Director: A. Van Putten

The master of accounting (MAcc) degree is designed to be completed in nine months or less, with flexible start dates of summer, fall, or spring. Students may also complete the program part-time. The program is well suited for students wanting to sharpen and broaden their knowledge of accounting topics and tools used in the accounting profession. It is also a good fit for those wanting to begin or advance their career in accounting in the public or private sector.

Students may apply to the MAcc program following successful completion of ACCT 301 or its equivalent. A cumulative grade point of average of at least a 3.0 is required to be admitted into this program.

Although law school applicants must have a college degree, there is no prescribed program or major specifically designed for students planning to enter law school. Like most colleges, Calvin does not offer a pre-law major, but rather a pre-law specialization within a student's chosen disciplinary major. Prospective law school applicants should complete the requirements for a bachelor of arts degree in their chosen major as prescribed in the core curriculum, taking advantage of the opportunities provided therein to acquire skills, knowledge, and insights useful for the practice of law. The pre-law advisor, M. Hessler (Politics and Economics) and J. Westra (Politics and Economics), can help students to plan programs and select courses that provide good preparation for law school. The pre-law advisor also can help to guide students through the processes of identifying law as a calling, preparing for the Law School Admission Test (LSAT), and applying to law schools. Pre-law students should declare their interest in the pre-law specialization during academic advising and should plan to attend the pre-law information sessions that are held at the beginning of each fall semester. Pre-law students normally take the LSAT In the spring of their junior year and should apply for admission to law school during the fall of their senior year.

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",S,1717,"School of Health",13718,,Non-Degree,PRMED,"Pre-professional: Medicine, Dentistry, and Veterinary","

Students planning to apply to medical or dental schools should consult the School of Health advising office (pre-health@calvin.edu). Those planning to apply to veterinary schools should consult W. Miller (Biology). Students should also note the university Core requirements. For basic information regarding timelines, requirements, etc., pre-medical, pre-dental, and pre-veterinary students should consult the pre-med/dental/vet website using the A-Z index on Calvin's home page.

A student may select nearly any major concentration and still meet the entrance requirements for all medical, dental, and veterinary schools. However, nationwide the majority of the applicants to medical, dental, and veterinary schools are science majors.

Pre-medical students must balance the requirements for the Medical College Admission Test (MCAT) with the requirements for medical schools. The MCAT was revised in 2015, and many medical schools have a variety of prerequisite courses, specifically with the organic chemistry requirement (one vs. two semesters). The pre-dental prerequisites include the same courses as pre-med. We continue to recommend that students take CHEM 101-CHEM 102; CHEM 241-CHEM 242; PHYS 221- PHYS 222; STAT 143, STAT 145, or PSYC 255; PSYC 151; SOC 151; BIOL 141 or BIOL 161; and BIOL 206 or BIOL 331. Some schools require a 300-level biology course. One semester of calculus (MATH 132, MATH 161, or MATH 162) is recommended, but it is required by very few schools. Pre-med students should review college math requirements for medical schools, as some require 6-8 credits of college math. Some dental schools require microbiology (BIOL 207 or BIOL 336).

Because a few schools have unique requirements, students should consult with the School of Health advising office (pre-health@calvin.edu) to determine specific requirements of the schools to which they intend to apply.

Pre-medical and pre-dental students take their Medical College Admissions Test (MCAT) or Dental Admissions Test (DAT) once they have completed the recommended coursework and determined when they have the time to fully prepare for these tests. The timing of these tests depends on when the student intends to apply. Students should meet with the pre-health advisors to learn more about shadowing and observation hour requirements, clinical experience, and other activities that strengthen their application. Calvin offers an elective course that identifies many of these requirements: IDIS 106 - Introduction to Health Professions.

Pre-veterinary students should take the following courses: 2-3 semesters of general biology (BIOL 160 and BIOL 161 for biology majors or BIOL 141, BIOL 205, and BIOL 206 for other majors); CHEM 101-CHEM 102; CHEM 241-CHEM 242; PHYS 221-PHYS 222; STAT 143 or STAT 145; MATH 132, MATH 161, or MATH 162; ENGL 101; 2 semesters of social science; and 2 semesters of arts/humanities. Recommended biology electives (that are required by some programs) include microbiology (BIOL 207 or BIOL 336), genetics (BIOL 321), anatomy (BIOL 205 or BIOL 323), and cell physiology (BIOL 335). Public speaking (COMM 101) and nutrition (HE 254) are also required by some veterinary programs, and some students will need to take an online animal nutrition course from another university. Students should consult with W. Miller to determine specific requirements for the programs to which they plan to apply. Some veterinary programs require that the prerequisite courses be completed at the time of application to their program.

Pre-veterinary students may be required to take the Graduate Record Exam (GRE); however, many programs have dropped this testing requirement. Students should meet with the pre-veterinary advisor to learn more about shadowing and observation hour requirements, animal experience, and other activities that strengthen their application.

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",S,1717,"School of Health",13720,,Non-Degree,PRPHA,"Pre-professional: Pharmacy","

Students interested in a career in pharmacy will complete at least two years at Calvin before transferring to a college of pharmacy to complete four additional years of study culminating in a doctor of pharmacy (PharmD) degree. Many schools now prefer that students complete a BS or BA degree before enrolling in pharmacy school. Pre-pharmacy course requirements of the pharmacy schools vary greatly. Some schools do not accept advanced placement credits. Students should carefully consult the American Association of Colleges of Pharmacy website (www.aacp.org) and the websites for the pharmacy schools to which they intend to apply to plan an appropriate course of study. The School of Health advising office (pre-health@calvin.edu) will assist students in planning a pre-pharmacy curriculum, which most students take in the context of a chemistry or biochemistry major. A few pharmacy schools require the Pharmacy College Admission Test (PCAT), which should be taken in the summer once students have completed the recommended course work and determined when they have the time to fully prepare for these tests. The timing of these tests depends on when the student intends to apply. Students should meet with the pre-health advisors to learn more about shadowing and other activities that strengthen their application. Calvin offers an elective course that identifies many of these requirements: IDIS 106 - Introduction to Health Professions.

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",S,1717,"School of Health",13721,,Non-Degree,PRPA,"Pre-professional: Physician Assistant","

Students who would like to practice medicine under the supervision of a licensed physician should consider becoming a physician assistant (PA). A physician assistant can record medical histories, perform physical examinations, make diagnoses, counsel patients, order and administer laboratory tests, assist in surgery, set fractures, and prescribe drugs. Each graduate program determines its prerequisite courses, and students should consult with the School of Health advising office (pre-health@calvin.edu) for advice about preparatory courses and hours of direct patient care required by particular clinical training programs. Some PA programs require the prerequisite courses to have been completed at the time of application to their program.

Students can major in any discipline as long as they complete the prerequisite courses for the graduate program to which they intend to apply. Students may be required to take either the GRE (Graduate Record Exam) or the PACAT (PA College Admission Test). The timing of these tests will depend on coursework and/or when the student plans to apply to PA programs.

","Pre-professional: Physician Assistant

Finance is the study of how companies obtain funds needed for future growth and how people invest funds for future consumption. It includes business finance and personal finance. Finance deals with the concepts of time, cash flows, and risk, and how they are interrelated. It also deals with how resources are spent and budgeted. Finance is about the methods and the prices at which financial resources are exchanged, and about how this exchange impacts the decisions companies and individuals make.

This program may not be combined with the business minor. No more than two majors from the School of Business may be obtained.

Calvin's bachelor of arts in biochemistry is intended for students who anticipate careers that would benefit from a strong background in biochemistry. Biochemistry students at Calvin explore how to think about the world from a chemical perspective and how to use chemical knowledge as they engage the world. The BA degree in biochemistry provides a rigorous education in chemical and biochemical concepts while giving students flexibility to specialize in additional disciplines or to obtain clinical volunteer experience. The BA program is designed to pair well with an additional major. It may also be a good fit for students with pre-medicine, pre-pharmacy, pre-dental, and other pre-health professional interests, who could pair it with internship or clinical experience. The BA in biochemistry will help equip students in areas such as

  • neuroscience (double major in psychology);
• art restoration or scientific illustration (double major in art);
• data modeling or information management (double major in computer science); and
• policy or politics (double major in political science).

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1693,Engineering,13744,Minor,Non-Degree,BIOMD.MIN,"Biomedical Engineering Minor","

Biomedical engineering is a broad field that features many different types of engineering expertise working together to find solutions to address human health needs. This minor will prepare students for entry-level work or graduate school in the biomedical engineering field. As a biomedical engineer or biomedical technologist, you will be prepared to work on multidisciplinary teams alongside scientists, engineers, patients, nurses, and physicians to develop products, processes, or systems that can alleviate suffering and restore function and dignity. The biomedical engineering minor will contribute toward preparation for jobs involving a blend of research and development as well as experimentation and data analysis, as well as interacting collaboratively with regulatory bodies to bring innovative products to the market, or researching new materials, technologies, and processes that will enable others to unlock solutions for years to come.

","Biomedical Engineering Minor

Applicants for the MS in Geographic Information Science who have successfully completed the equivalent courses for the GIS certificate (GEO 560, GEO 561, GEO 562) at another accredited academic institution may be eligible for advanced standing. ,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1712,"Politics, Philosophy, and Economics",13746,Major,"Bachelor of Arts",PPE.MAJ,"Politics, Philosophy, and Economics, BA","

The PPE major provides a coherent and flexible interdisciplinary course of study, bridging gaps between economics and political science as social sciences and philosophy as one of the humanities. It is designed for students whose interests do not fit neatly into any one of the three disciplines, helping them to bring philosophical insights to bear on empirical social science research and use social science methods to measure philosophical claims about foundational concepts, such as equality and justice. The program works with these three disciplines within the framework of faith, emphasizing Christian calling and vocation and a demonstrated commitment to civil and informed public discourse and policy. The program provides structure within this flexibility through foundational and intermediate courses in each of the disciplines, a capstone course that integrates all three PPE disciplines and Christian faith, and common cocurricular experiences throughout the program.

This program may not be combined with the economics major, BS; economics major, BA; economics minor; political science major; or political science minor programs.

","Politics, Philosophy, and Economics, BA

Students must maintain a 2.0 in psychology courses in order to graduate with a major or minor in psychology.
Psychology majors who are also ministry minors are advised to take PSYC 201.
Students should ordinarily take PSYC 255 during their sophomore year and PSYC 256 in the semester following completion of PSYC 255. Students may not take PSYC 255 and PSYC 256 simultaneously.

When possible, students are encouraged to postpone taking courses numbered 330 or above until after the completion of PSYC 256. PSYC 399 is a course that may be taken when the student has completed a majority of the psychology courses required for the psychology major.

Students intending to do doctoral work in psychology are strongly encouraged to take PSYC 356 during their junior or senior year. When selecting courses, students are encouraged to follow the recommended four-year plan for the general psychology major or the plan for majors with a neuroscience concentration. A four-year plan is available in the psychology office.

The psychology department offers a bachelor of arts (BA) degree in psychology and a bachelor of science (BS) degree in psychology. The BS course of study has stronger quantitative and research components. It is recommended that students intent on graduate study in psychology complete the coursework for the BS degree. 

","Psychology Major, BS

The online master of of science in exercise science offers concentrations in performance and sport science and health and exercise rehabilitation. The program may be completed on a part-time or full-time basis; the minimum amount of time for completion is 12 months. All students complete seven foundational courses involving essential exercise science knowledge and skills in research and health. Students will complete three additional courses in their chosen concentration that target specific skills in either performance and sport science or health and exercise rehabilitation. Although both concentrations may be completed, students receive only one MS credential.

Students who choose the performance and sport science concentration will become specialists in performance, strength, and conditioning of more elite athletes and special training regimens for the purpose of performance. Whether the client be an individual athlete, a team, or a small group of individuals with similar goals, the student will be well educated in advanced sport science protocols.

Application requirements include a bachelor's degree with a minimum cumulative GPA of 3.0, two reference letters, and a personal statement. More information is available on the department's website.

","Exercise Science: Performance and Sport Science Concentration, MS

The online master of science in exercise science offers concentrations in performance and sport science and health and exercise rehabilitation. The program may be completed on a part-time or full-time basis; the minimum amount of time for completion is 12 months. All students complete seven foundational courses involving essential exercise science knowledge and skills in research and health. Students will complete three additional courses in their chosen concentration that target specific skills in either performance and sport science or health rehabilitation. Although both concentrations may be completed, students receive only one MS credential.

Students who choose the health and exercise rehabilitation concentration will be equipped to help individuals develop achievable and measurable goals, motivate them to improve health in a safe manner, and help them maintain lasting healthy lifestyle change. Obesity-related conditions include heart disease, stroke, type 2 diabetes, and certain types of cancer, which are some of the leading causes of preventable, premature death. The health and exercise rehabilitation concentration will equip students with skills to help individuals make lasting positive changes in health outcomes.

Application requirements include a bachelor's degree with a minimum cumulative GPA of 3.0, two reference letters, and a personal statement. More information is available on the department's website.

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",S,1716,"School of Business",13760,Master's,"Master of Business Administration",MBA.BUSAD,"Business Administration, MBA","

Program director: L. Van Drunen

The Calvin MBA develops faithful leaders and innovators while also providing business essentials and allowing electives from many different disciplines. In most courses students learn experientially by engaging with real companies and professionals.

The MBA program is mostly taught asynchronous and online, which allows students flexibility to pursue the MBA as their life allows. Learning in community is a hallmark of the Calvin MBA, which is supported with student cohorts, some synchronous sessions in each course, and two 1-week summer residencies on Calvin's campus.

The MBA program comprises fifteen courses totaling 36 semester hours, which can be completed in as little as sixteen full-time months or in twenty-three part-time months. The heart of the MBA program is a set of leadership and innovation courses totaling 15 hours taken over thirteen months with a cohort of fellow MBA students. In addition to those courses, MBA students take up to 12 hours of business essentials courses. Some or all of the business essentials courses may be waived upon admission if a student has completed similar undergraduate courses with at least a B within the past five years. If more than two such courses are waived, additional electives must be taken so that MBA courses total at least 30 hours. Finally, students must take 7 to 9 hours of electives. Working with an advisor, students may optionally organize electives to focus in areas such as marketing, finance, and applied communication.

Prior to taking any courses required for the Calvin MBA, students must be admitted to Calvin's MBA graduate program or another Calvin graduate program.

","Business Administration, MBA

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",S,1716,"School of Business",13773,Major,"Bachelor of Science",BS.OPSCM,"Operations and Supply Chain Management, BS","

People working in operations and supply chain are concerned with designing, overseeing, and controlling how goods and services are produced, allocated and distributed. They manage the process that converts inputs (in the forms of material, labor, and energy) into outputs (in the form of goods and services). People in this field are typically team-oriented individuals who have an interest in manufacturing, logistics, distribution, or retail careers. They ensure a business's operations are both efficient and effective and promote wise and sustainable stewardship of God-given resources.

This program may not be combined with the business minor. No more than two majors from the School of Business may be obtained.

","Operations and Supply Chain Management, BS

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",S,1716,"School of Business",13774,"Certificate: Undergraduate",Non-Degree,CERT.GLBL,"Global Business, Certificate","

Students earn a global business certificate if they complete the following requirements:

column2: • A major in an undergraduate business program.
• Four courses totaling twelve or more credit hours from the categories below.
• A summative essay with guided prompts and a published rubric.
• Review and approval of the above from the global business certificate coordinator.

column1: The required four courses totaling 12 or more semester hours will be selected by the student from the following three categories and provided to the global business certificate coordinator for approval:

column2: • CATEGORY 1: Off-Campus Course in Business or closely related area. Students must take one course in category 1 and could take up to two courses in this category.

• CATEGORY 2: Social Science Course with an international, cross-cultural, or global focus. Students must take one course in category 2 and could take up to two courses in this category. These may be Core courses tagged for Global Regions and Cultures or other approved social science courses including approved social science courses during a Calvin off-campus semester program. A list of pre-approved courses is available from the global business certificate coordinator.

• CATEGORY 3: Humanities Course with an international, cross-cultural, or global focus. Students do not need to take a category 3 course but may take up to two courses in this category. These may be Core courses tagged for Global Regions and Cultures or other approved humanities courses including approved humanities courses during a Calvin off-campus semester program. A list of pre-approved courses is available from the global business certificate coordinator.

column1:
For additional details, please contact the School of Business global business certificate coordinator.

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1679,Communication,13775,Major,"Bachelor of Arts",CASSC.MAJ,"Strategic Communication, BA","

The Department of Communication offers the strategic communication major, a program rooted in the liberal arts that helps students build the necessary faculties to be highly effective communicators in their professions and in public life. Strategic communication courses teach students to understand their listeners, articulate ideas clearly, argue logically, listen carefully, communicate ethically, unify groups of people under a common goal, and analyze and evaluate how communication works in specific contexts.  Moreover, students learn to use the gift of communication as a means of glorifying God, advocating for what is true and just, and bridging divisions between people. Strategic communication alumni choose careers in public relations, advertising, social media, content creation, law, ministry, education, journalism, and politics.

This program may not be combined with the sports communication major program.

","Strategic Communication, BA

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",S,1722,"School of Graduate and Continuing Studies",13778,"Certificate: Undergraduate",Non-Degree,CERT.CI,"FROZEN 23/FA: Cultural Intelligence for Christian Leaders, Certificate","

The cultural values and intercultural learning certificate program nurtures congregational and ministerial leaders and Christian educators with the knowledge, skills, and commitment to a vocation of leading with cultural competency and an appreciation of the cultural diversity of the body of Christ. Grounded in the Reformed Christian perspective of culturally competent and intelligent leadership, the program exposes participants to the tapestry of cultural values, fosters best practices of intercultural learning and interaction, and cultivates a pastoral mindset among congregational and ministry leaders.

This graduate certificate program requires the completion of four foundational courses (8 semester hours) and one capstone course (2 semester hours) where students integrate the learning from the foundations courses and create a visionary plan of application in their own setting.

The courses will include both asynchronous community learning and periodic synchronous interactions.

","FROZEN 23/FA: Cultural Intelligence for Christian Leaders, Certificate

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",S,1717,"School of Health",13779,,Non-Degree,,"Health Science","

Healthcare delivery is a dynamic and complex endeavor that relies on graduates who are eager to impart meaningful change in the world around them. Students pursuing health science majors may matriculate as nurses, community educators, healthcare communicators, or within any of dozens of other roles within healthcare. While the possibilities are diverse, the importance of understanding and being able to work with other practitioners within this system has become a key competency for graduates.

Students pursuing a major in the health sciences will complete eight foundational courses in chemistry, biology, psychology, sociology, and/or statistics before completing additional coursework in their chosen field of study. In this way, the health science foundational courses will serve as a gateway to a range of fields within the health sciences, allowing students to move easily between health science majors, as they discern their vocational calling.

Upon completion of the health science foundational courses, students will continue to major in nursing, kinesiology (exercise science) or pre-occupational therapy. Students will be advised by faculty in these departments and will need to meet the specifications for graduation, beyond the health-science foundational courses, outlined by each program.

","Health Science

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",S,1716,"School of Business",13805,Major,"Bachelor of Arts",ENSHP.MAJ,"Entrepreneurship, BA","

Entrepreneurial skills are key to innovation and growth in society, whether in a startup business, a nonprofit, or a Fortune 500 company. In this major, students foster an entrepreneurial mindset through both in-classroom and out-in-the-world learning. Simultaneously, they will also grow in their understanding of how God can use organizations to affect positive change in the world. By deepening their understanding of others and developing skills to understand and frame problems and develop testable solutions, students will graduate with insights and abilities that are valuable for life-long learning and service.

This program may not be combined with the business minor program. No more than two majors from the School of Business may be obtained.

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",S,1716,"School of Business",13806,Major,"Bachelor of Science",ANLYT.MAJ,"Business Analytics, BS","

The BS in business analytics prepares graduates to structure business problems in ways that can be solved by data-science, information-technology, or machine learning applications; and to plan, organize, and lead organizational change projects that deliver such solutions. These ""business analysts"" work with data scientists and computer scientists, and therefore require knowledge and skills in organizational information ecosystems, algorithmic logic, database queries, predictive analytics, and the translation of business rules into software code. However, they also bring complementary business acumen, people management, and organizational change skills, including process design, collaborative project management, and negotiation, as well as an understanding of the business imperatives driving technological adoption.

Business analysts often begin their careers coordinating development projects, whether for the in-house information technology departments of large organizations, for software application development companies, or for systems integration and market intelligence consultancies. Further, analysts act as the communication bridge between the business and technology communities with strong presentation and leadership skills.

Students attracted to business, who also have abilities in systems thinking and data-driven inquiry, would be good candidates for this program. Students who are attracted to technology and have abilities in collaborative business problem-solving would also be good candidates for this program.

This program may not be combined with the business minor or information systems minor programs. No more than two majors from the School of Business may be obtained.

","Business Analytics, BS

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1690,"Data Science",13808,Master's,"Master of Science",MS_DATA,"INACTIVE, to be reviewed 11/2026: Data Science, MS","

The data science MS degree is designed to be completed in three to six semesters. The program is suited for students who wish to gain technical expertise in data science in a context that also thoughtfully reflects on the impacts this work has on a wide variety of stakeholders. Graduates of the program will be prepared for a wide range of professional work in the public or private sector.

Students may apply to the program having successfully completed a four-year degree at an accredited academic institution. A cumulative grade point average of at least 3.0 in the last two years of undergraduate education is required to be admitted to the program. Students should have completed at least one course in each of calculus, statistics, and computer science (programming), but it may be possible to begin the program while completing these prerequisites.

Completion of the program requires 35 hours of coursework. Up to 8 of these hours may be met through coursework completed as part of another program or at a different institution.

","INACTIVE, to be reviewed 11/2026: Data Science, MS

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1692,Education,13810,Master's,"Master of Arts",MAT_MAT,"Master of Arts in Teaching, MAT","

The master of arts in teaching (MAT) program provides an accelerated pathway to initial teacher certification and a master's degree for post-baccalaureate candidates whose degrees include certifiable majors or minors. The program is designed for career-changers, those re-entering the workforce, and recent bachelor's degree graduates. Students complete a condensed 12-month cohort program and benefit from school internship placements alongside intensive coursework. The program is characterized by 1) integration of academic work with an ongoing field experience; 2) programmatic coherence throughout the academic courses and from coursework to field work; 3) systematic study of classroom practices; 4) individual, mentor teacher, and instructor-observed evaluation. Upon successful completion of the program, candidates earn a master of arts in teaching (MAT) degree and a Michigan Standard Teaching Certificate qualifying them to teach middle and high school grades.

column2: • Interest in working with middle or high school students
• Bachelor's degree with an approved certifiable area from a regionally-accredited college or university.
• Grade point average of 2.8 or greater. Graduate coursework and/or work history will be considered.

column3: • A professional essay explaining why the candidate has chosen teaching as a profession and why they have selected Calvin's MAT program for professional preparation.
• Submission of two letters of professional recommendation.
• Official transcripts from all colleges and universities attended.

column2: • An interview with members of the MAT team, which will be scheduled after all application documents are submitted.

• Attempt the appropriate Michigan Test for Teacher Certification subject test. Applicants who do not pass the first attempt may enter the program with the understanding that they must retake and pass the test for teacher certification.

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1721,"Honors and Collegiate Scholars",13817,Program,Non-Degree,HONORS,"Honors Scholars Program","

The Honors Scholars Program offers Calvin University students the opportunity for advanced study in both the liberal arts and their selected field of study in a community of engaged, bold, and curious learners.

Incoming students who are interested in exploring the intersections between the humanities, natural sciences, and social sciences; desire to learn in a challenging and encouraging environment with invested peers and faculty; and want to develop the skills of a scholar by working alongside a faculty mentor in their field of study are encouraged to apply to the Calvin Honors Scholars Program. Application materials can be found on Calvin's Honors and Collegiate Scholars website.

Each year, a cohort of 48 students will be invited to participate in the Honors Scholars Program. Students in the cohort will fulfill 18 credits of their Calvin Core course requirements in alternative Honors Scholars Core courses.

To graduate ""with Honors,"" a student must complete the courses shown above and achieve an overall GPA of 3.50. Students must also submit an ""Application to Graduate with Honors"" to the Honors and Collegiate Scholars Office at the beginning of their final year.

For further information, contact the director (A. Wilstermann) or associate director (C. Hanson) of the Honors and Collegiate Scholars Program.

","Honors Scholars Program

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1713,"Visual and Performing Arts",13818,Major,"Bachelor of Music Education",BME_MUSC_PK12,"Music Education PK-12, BME","

This program prepares students to teach music in PK-12 school settings. Students take courses in music theory and musicianship, music technology, music literature and history, and music education. Students gain confidence as musicians by taking lessons in their principal area of performance, participating in a wide range of ensemble experiences, and presenting a recital. Music Education majors develop teaching skills in dedicated classes and in guided teaching experiences, including a full semester student teaching assignment. Throughout the program, students learn how Christian faith commitments and practices impact teaching and learning in the general music classroom as they also develop skills to build and lead instrumental and choral programs.

For admission to the BME program, students must present an entrance audition and take a music theory test. (The music theory test is waived for students with transfer credit or a minimum score of 4 on the AP Music Theory exam.)

","Music Education PK-12, BME

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1691,Economics,13820,Major,"Bachelor of Arts",ECON.MAJ.BA,"Economics Major, BA","

The bachelor of arts (BA) in economics helps students to develop a clear and logical way of thinking about real-world issues such as unemployment, inflation, taxes, healthcare, poverty, inequality, pollution, crime, and international trade. Students in the program learn to synthesize large amounts of information and to think logically while gaining high-demand skills in problem-solving and communication. They also learn to identify the costs and the benefits of a decision with scarce resources. Students in the program graduate with skills to complement successful careers in a wide range of fields, including finance, consulting, banking, accounting, law, nonprofits, politics, diplomacy, government and public policy. The BA in economics is a broad and flexible program of study. It works particularly well for students who pursue multiple majors or minors, who start the economics major later in their undergraduate careers, or who wish to study economics using an analytical but not heavily quantitative approach.

This program may not be combined with the politics, philosophy, and economics major program.

Students will learn the relationship between sports and culture and how communication functions within sports organizations and society. Students will develop a strong theoretical and critical understanding of communication and will use this knowledge to create effective messages for their target audiences. Students also will develop the ability to communicate effectively through various media, adapting to the fast-paced changes in communication technology. In multiple courses and in the sports communication internship, students will develop the necessary professional skills to succeed in a variety of sports communication careers in social media, public relations, advertising, journalism, broadcasting, or community relations.

In collaboration with the School of Business, the School of Graduate and Continuing Studies offers a BA in Applied Leadership. The program prepares adult learners who are seeking a degree-completion program to move into areas of greater responsibility and accountability in their organizations through an interdisciplinary curriculum that explores human behavior and its application to leadership. This curriculum is rooted in Christian virtues and practices and includes relevant theory and principles.

In addition to the requirements of the major, students also must satisfy the requirements of the degree-completion Core curriculum.

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1708,"Sociology and Social Work",13827,Master's,"Master of Social Work",,"Social Work, MSW","

The master of social work (MSW) is designed for students who want to both deepen and broaden their understanding of the social work field in preparation for a career of service as an advanced professional social worker. The MSW program at Calvin is informed by the National Association of Social Workers (NASW) code of ethics, the integration of faith and learning, dependence on evidence-based practices, and engagement with scholarly research. MSW students at Calvin will explore historical, global, environmental, and cultural influences as they prepare to competently serve others by advancing the well-being (flourishing) of individuals, families, and systems of in a variety of clinical and macrolevel settings.
Applicants who have earned a BSW from a CSWE-accredited program within the past seven years may be eligible for advanced standing.

Upon completion of the program, students will be prepared to engage in clinical and/or macro advanced social work practice.

","Social Work, MSW

,25,"2026-2027 DRAFT Catalog",T,1688,"Computer Science",13832,"Certificate: Undergraduate",Non-Degree,,"Cybersecurity, Certificate","

The cybersecurity certificate equips students with both the principles and the practical skills for protecting information, end hosts, and networks from cyber threats. The program offers an exposure to a range of cybersecurity problems confronting us today and, at the same time, provides an in-depth understanding of how the principles (e.g., of cryptography) can be applied to secure computers and networks. Students will be given opportunities to practice what they learn through hands-on labs and projects, and internships. All of the above are designed to serve the foundation for furthering studies at the graduate levels as well as for becoming security professionals in various business sectors and government agencies.

","Cybersecurity, Certificate

The earth and environmental science major serves as a foundation for a variety of scientific careers and has two concentrations: environmental science and geoscience. Students in the environmental science concentration take advanced coursework in environmental history, environmental law, and plant and wildlife biology, and pursue careers as park naturalists, conservation officers, environmental educators, and environmental consultants.

","Earth and Environmental Science, BS: Environmental Science Concentration
